stimulus_text:
Elena stood at the edge of the school pool, her toes curled over the cold tiles. The regional swimming carnival was in twenty minutes, and her stomach felt like it was full of moths instead of butterflies. She had trained every morning before school for six weeks, but that seemed to matter less now the marker called her name for the 100-metre freestyle.

"You don't have to win," her brother Marcus said, appearing with a towel slung over his shoulder. "You just have to swim your own race."

Elena almost laughed. Easy for him to say — he'd already won two ribbons that morning. Her own cap was faded and slightly too big, a hand-me-down from Marcus's old kit bag, next to swimmers from bigger schools with matching caps.

"What if I come last?" she asked quietly.

Marcus crouched so he was level with her. "Then you'll have swum your best race and come last. That's still better than not racing at all." He tapped her goggles until they sat straight. "Besides, you shaved four seconds off your time last week. Nobody here has done that."

The starter's voice crackled over the loudspeaker. Elena's legs felt heavy as she climbed onto the block, but she found herself thinking about the countless mornings she'd pushed off this wall before the sun was even up.

The whistle blew. Elena dived cleanly, the cold water wrapping around her like something almost familiar. She found her rhythm by the second lap, arms slicing forward in a pattern she no longer needed to think about, the crowd's roar growing louder as she neared the wall.

When she touched the tiles and pulled off her goggles, she scanned the board, almost afraid to look. Third place. Not first, but a personal best by nearly three seconds. Marcus was already grinning, holding up a soggy towel like a trophy.

"Told you," he said. "You raced your own race."

Elena climbed out, legs shaking, but for the first time all morning, the moths in her stomach had turned into something closer to pride.

stimulus_title: The Letterbox at the End of the Lane
stimulus_text:
The Letterbox at the End of the Lane

Rusty red and leaning slightly,
half-hidden by the wattle tree,
the letterbox waits every morning
for whatever the world might send to me.

Sometimes it holds a birthday card,
sometimes a bill, sometimes just leaves,
but I still run the gravel path
before my mother even believes.

I open it slow, like a secret door,
and even on the days it's bare,
I like to think the lane remembers
every letter that once lived there.

stimulus_text: Bandicoots are small, nocturnal marsupials found across parts of Australia. They use their long, pointed noses to dig cone-shaped holes in the soil while searching for insects, worms and roots to eat. A single bandicoot can dig dozens of these small holes in one night, which helps to aerate garden soil and control insect pests naturally. Because they are shy and rarely seen during the day, many people do not realise bandicoots are living nearby until they spot the tell-tale holes in their lawn the next morning. Several bandicoot species are now considered vulnerable due to habitat loss, so wildlife groups encourage households to leave dense, low shrubs in gardens where bandicoots can safely shelter.

stimulus_text:
*In this extract from a memoir, the narrator remembers the last week of the summer holidays, spent helping at her grandfather's boatshed on the south coast of New South Wales.*

The alarm rasped at a quarter to five, and I lay still for a moment, listening to the sea breathing in the dark. Mum had said I didn't have to go. Grandpa never asked twice — that was not his way — but on the first morning of the holidays I had promised him a week, and in our family a promise was not a decoration.

The track down to Currawong Bay took eight minutes at a trot, past the sleeping caravan park and the fig tree full of grumbling flying foxes. The boatshed crouched at the end of the jetty like an old grey cat, its tin roof still holding yesterday's warmth. Grandpa was already there. He was always already there.

"Ropes first," he said, without turning around. He never said good morning. Mornings, to Grandpa, were not good or bad; they were simply hours that should not be wasted.

I hauled the stiff coils down from their hooks while he **coaxed** the old kerosene lamp into life, muttering to it in Vietnamese the way other people mutter to cats. My fingers were clumsy with cold. His moved without thinking, and the knots he left behind were small and neat; mine looked like a seagull's nest.

We bailed out the dinghies. We checked the oars of the six hire boats, lying in their rack like sardines in a tin. We chalked the day's prices on the blackboard — he had not raised them in four years, though Mum nagged him about it every Sunday lunch. Forty-one years he had run that shed, and the routine had worn itself into him like the groove in the jetty plank where the handcart always rolled.

Halfway through the morning we found a white envelope wedged under the door. Grandpa read the first line, then folded it into his shirt pocket, and for the rest of the hour he worked without his usual muttering, tying and untying the same mooring line three times. I didn't ask. You didn't ask Grandpa things; you waited, the way you waited for the tide.

The sun came up while I was scrubbing gull mess off the ticket window. It did not rise so much as arrive, all at once, laying a road of copper light across the bay, and every dinghy and every wet rope turned briefly to gold. Grandpa stopped. He stood with the lamp in his hand — pale and pointless now — and looked out at the water for a long time.

"Not bad," he said at last.

From Grandpa, that was a speech.

By seven the first fishermen were shuffling down the jetty, and I found, to my surprise, that I was sorry the quiet was ending. On the first morning I had counted the minutes until breakfast. Now I chalked the board without being asked, and I had stopped minding the smell of kerosene, which no longer seemed like a smell at all, but simply the smell of the shed, of Grandpa, and of the hour before the world woke up.

Tonight I climb the hundred and twelve stairs
for the last time that counts,
my palm on the brass rail worn to butter

stimulus_title: The Quiet Achievers of the Sea Floor
stimulus_text:
Below the glitter of Australia's coastal bays, past the moored dinghies and the paddleboards, lie some of the hardest-working landscapes in the country. Seagrass meadows — vast underwater paddocks of ribbon weed and strapweed — sway in the current from Shark Bay in the west to the lakes of eastern Victoria. They are easy to overlook. Yet scientists now argue that these unassuming plants may be among our most valuable allies in the fight against climate change.

Seagrasses are not seaweeds. They are true flowering plants, descendants of land plants that drifted back into the sea millions of years ago, complete with roots, veins and tiny flowers. As they grow, they draw carbon dioxide from the water and lock it into their leaves and, more importantly, into the tangled mat of roots and mud beneath them. "A healthy meadow buries carbon up to thirty-five times faster than the same area of tropical rainforest," says Dr Priya Raman, a marine ecologist at the Coastal Futures Institute in Adelaide. "And unlike a forest, which releases its carbon when it burns, a seagrass meadow can hold its store safely in the mud for thousands of years." Researchers call this hidden treasure "blue carbon".

Carbon storage is not the meadows' only service. Seagrasses also provide a nursery for young fish, seahorses and prawns, and their roots knit the seabed together, softening the punch of storm waves before they reach the shore. Yet the meadows are in trouble. Dredging for new marinas, the dragging chains of boat moorings and a string of marine heatwaves have stripped thousands of hectares from our coasts in the past two decades.

There is, however, cautious good news. At Bass Landing, Professor Callum Whitford of the Southern Seagrass Restoration Project leads teams of volunteer divers who collect seagrass fruit each summer, seal the seeds inside small hessian bags of sand, and anchor the bags to bare patches of seabed. "We can't plant a meadow the way you plant a paddock," Professor Whitford admits. "What we can do is give each seed a safe anchor, then let the sea do the rest." Early trials have seen new shoots spreading within two years.

For Dr Raman, the message is simple. "When people picture the fight against climate change, they picture solar panels and wind farms," she says. "They should also picture a quiet green paddock, two metres under water, doing its work with no applause."

stimulus_text:
For most of recorded history, the fastest way to send a message across a war zone was to tie it to a bird and hope for the best. The humble pigeon, a creature now widely (1) ____ as a feathered nuisance, was once among the most (2) ____ recruits in any army. Generals paid small fortunes for proven fliers, and champion birds were (3) ____ across the country under armed guard, like tiny grey celebrities.

The method itself was disarmingly simple. A pigeon raised in one loft, when (4) ____ hundreds of kilometres from home, will fly straight back with almost unsettling determination. Messages were rolled into tiny canisters and (5) ____ to the bird's leg; the pigeon supplied the navigation, the speed and, occasionally, the heroics. One famous wartime bird completed its delivery despite serious injuries, an effort for which it was (6) ____ a medal — an honour most human soldiers waited years to receive.

Scientists still (7) ____ over exactly how the birds find their way. Magnetic fields, the angle of the sun and even a keen sense of smell have all been proposed. Whatever the trick, the record is hard to (8) ____: for two thousand years, nothing delivered a message more reliably. The next pigeon that eyes your sandwich may descend from decorated veterans.

stimulus_text:
**Extract A** — from *Learning to Fall Upwards*, a memoir by Priya Raman

The instructor, Sal, had a habit of saying 'easy' about things that were not. 'Running off a mountain is easy,' she said, tightening my harness. 'It's the standing still beforehand that's hard.'

She was right, though I didn't admit it. My legs made the decision before my brain had finished objecting; three strides, then a fourth that landed on nothing. The ground folded away beneath me, and the ridge that had seemed enormous all morning shrank to a wrinkle of brown corduroy.

I had expected noise — roaring wind, my own hammering heart. Instead there was a silence so complete it felt almost polite, broken only by the wing rustling above me like a paper bag. Below, the paddocks slid past in no particular hurry.

I remember thinking that I had spent thirty-nine years assuming the air was empty. It isn't. It has hills and valleys of its own, invisible ones; the glider climbed some and stumbled down others. When a rising column caught us, and my stomach dropped even as we lifted, I laughed out loud. Sal's voice crackled through the radio strapped to my chest: 'Told you. Easy.'

**Extract B** — from an article in *Southern Skies* magazine

The wedge-tailed eagle, Australia's largest bird of prey, can stay aloft for hours while barely flapping its wings. Its secret is not strength but strategy: the bird is a master of the thermal, a rising column of warm air.

Thermals form when the sun heats the ground unevenly. A ploughed paddock or a sheet of bare rock warms faster than the bushland beside it, and the hot air above it rises like an invisible fountain. An eagle entering this fountain simply spreads its two-metre wings and circles, letting the moving air carry it upward. Glider pilots use exactly the same trick, and it is not unusual for a glider and an eagle to share a thermal, spiralling politely around one another.

Height, for an eagle, is capital to be spent. From a thousand metres it can survey several square kilometres of country for rabbits and carrion, then glide to the next thermal with hardly a wingbeat. Researchers fitting the birds with lightweight GPS units found some individuals travelling more than a hundred kilometres in a day while flapping for less than ten minutes. The rest, quite literally, was rising hot air.

stimulus_text:
**Extract A**

Eleanor Fairweather had been packing, in one way or another, since her twelfth birthday. The trunk at the foot of her bed had filled slowly over six years — a good coat, saved coins, letters of introduction begged from every respectable acquaintance — so that when the coach finally came rattling up the lane one grey October morning, there was nothing left to do but carry it down. Her mother stood at the gate with her mouth set in a firm line that was not quite disapproval. 'You will write,' she said. It was not a question. Eleanor promised, kissed her mother's cold cheek, and climbed up beside the driver. She did not look back at the house. She had discovered, somewhere in those six years of packing, that looking back was a habit best broken early.

**Extract B**

Every April, the short-tailed shearwater leaves its burrow on the islands of Bass Strait and does not touch land again for weeks. Its journey — a vast loop of some fifteen thousand kilometres to the seas off Alaska and back — is one of the longest migrations of any Australian bird. Remarkably, no parent shows the young the way. Adults depart first, leaving their plump, half-grown chicks behind in the burrows; when hunger finally drives the young birds out, they fly the entire route alone, guided by instincts written into them before hatching. Scientists believe the birds navigate using the stars, the Earth's magnetic field and the memory of smells carried on the wind. For a shearwater, leaving home is not a decision at all. It is a season, and it arrives whether the bird is ready or not.

**Extract C**

When I moved to Sydney for university, my grandmother told me I was not leaving Country, because Country is not a place you can leave. I am a Wiradjuri woman; the river I grew up beside runs through everything I do, even six hours' drive away. Still, that first year was hard in ways I had not expected. It was not the study, or the crowds, or even the homesickness exactly. It was the smallness of the sky between the buildings. I found my balance in unexpected places: a eucalypt in the university courtyard, phone calls home every Sunday, an Aboriginal student centre where nobody needed the river explained to them. Leaving, I have learned, is not the opposite of belonging. Some of us carry home with us, the way a river carries the rain that fell somewhere far upstream.

**Extract D**

So here's the thing nobody tells you about moving out at nineteen: you will pack the wrong stuff. I took my entire collection of novels (heavy, pointless) and forgot a can-opener (small, essential). My first night in the share house in Brunswick, my flatmate Duc and I attacked a tin of tomato soup with a screwdriver, laughing so hard we nearly gave up and ordered pizza. I'd planned the whole move in about a week, which Mum said was 'very on brand'. She cried at the door anyway, then texted me before I'd even reached the end of the street: 'You forgot the can-opener.' Mums know. Honestly, the move was a mess — the mattress didn't fit up the stairs, the fridge smelled like regret — but that chaotic soup dinner is still one of my favourite memories.

stimulus_text:
*The town of Wattle Bend has not seen real rain in three years. Nan sits on the verandah at dusk, watching the road that no cars come down anymore.*

The dust came first, the way it always did, sliding under the door in a thin red line that Nan swept back three times a day and lost to every evening. She had stopped counting the days without rain the way you stop counting anything that only makes you sad. The tank at the side of the house rang hollow when she knocked it with her knuckle, a sound like a church bell in an empty church, and she thought, not for the first time, that the whole of Wattle Bend was becoming an empty church.

Her grandson Kai was inside, drawing rivers. He drew them fat and blue across the backs of old bills, rivers with fish in them, rivers that curled around whole cities. He had never seen one. Born in the dry, raised in the dry, he drew water the way other children drew dragons -- something marvellous and half-believed.

Nan reached up to the shelf and took down the biscuit tin, the one with the faded kookaburra on the lid. Inside, wrapped in a handkerchief, was one curled photograph and a twist of paper heavy with seeds. The photograph showed a girl of nine standing knee-deep in a billabong, laughing, her dress soaked to the waist.

*She was that girl.* She remembered the day the way you remember a smell -- all at once and from everywhere. It had rained for a week when she was nine, a proper drenching wet that turned the paddocks to mirrors. Her own grandmother, old Pearl, had taken her down to the billabong and said, *Bindi*[1]*, the water always comes back. It goes away to teach us to wait, but it always comes back.* And little Nan had not believed her, not really, because a week of rain seemed like a thing that had always been and always would be. She had splashed and shrieked and thought the wet would last forever. It had not. But it had come back, that year and the next and the one after -- until, somewhere in the long middle of her life, it had started to forget the way.

Now she was the old woman by the water that wasn't there. She turned the photograph over in her fingers. Kai came out and leaned against her, smelling of pencil and dust.

"Nan, will it ever rain again?"

She almost said what she had always felt in the dry years -- *maybe not, love, maybe not for us.* The words were right there, grey and certain. But she looked at the boy's rivers spread out on the boards, all that blue he had never touched, and something in her turned over like soil.

"Yes," she said. "It will. It goes away to teach us to wait." She pressed the twist of seeds into his hand. "And when it does, we'll be ready to plant."

Kai looked at the seeds, then up at the dry, hard sky, and for the first time in a long time Nan let herself look up too, not to check for cloud, but simply because that was where the rain would come from, when it came. Beside her the boy began, very quietly, to hum.

[1] *Bindi*: an affectionate name; here, the grandmother's pet name for the child.

stimulus_text:
1  In the coastal desert of the Atacama, rain may not fall for years at a time, yet thick banks of fog roll in from the ocean almost every morning. A growing number of villages there have learned to harvest that fog for drinking water, using nothing more complicated than a large panel of net strung between two poles.

2  The idea is simple. As a fog bank drifts through the mesh, tiny water droplets suspended in the air catch on the fine mesh, merge into larger drops, and trickle down into a gutter. From the gutter the water runs along a pipe to a storage tank in the village below. A single net the size of a bedsheet can gather several buckets of clean water on a good foggy morning.

3  These modest panels have quietly changed daily life in the villages that use them. Dr Amara Okonkwo, a water engineer who has studied the nets for a decade, explains that families who once walked hours to fetch water can now collect it a short stroll from home. "The technology is humble," she says, "but the difference it makes to a household's day is enormous."

4  Not everyone is convinced the nets are a complete answer. Professor Liang Wei, a climate scientist, points out that fog is unreliable. "On a clear, windless week you may catch almost nothing," he warns. "A village cannot depend on fog alone; the nets work best alongside other water sources, not instead of them."

5  Even so, researchers agree the nets have a real future in dry, foggy regions from Chile to Morocco. They are cheap to build, easy to repair, and need no electricity to run. In a warming world where fresh water grows scarcer, a curtain of mesh that drinks the fog may prove to be one of the simplest good ideas of all.

question_text:
Ms Ferreira says: "No stallholder who sold out early stayed until closing time at the harvest market. Rosa stayed until closing time, so Rosa cannot have sold anything at all."

Which one of the following sentences shows the mistake Ms Ferreira has made?
option_a: Just because Rosa stayed until closing time, it does not mean that she enjoyed the market.
option_b: Just because no stallholder who sold out early stayed until closing, it does not mean that the market closed late.
option_c: Just because a stallholder sold out early, it does not mean that they went straight home.
option_d: Just because Rosa did not sell out early, it does not mean that she sold nothing at all.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Whether Rosa enjoyed the market is beside the point; the flaw concerns what her staying late proves about her sales.","B":"The closing time of the market is not part of Ms Ferreira's reasoning.","C":"What early sellers did after selling out is irrelevant to the conclusion about Rosa.","D":"Correct: From the facts we can only conclude that Rosa did not sell out early; jumping from that to \"she sold nothing at all\" is the overreach in the argument."}
worked_solution: From the given rule, anyone who sold out early left before closing, so Rosa, who stayed to closing, cannot have sold out early - that much is valid. Ms Ferreira then leaps much further, concluding Rosa sold nothing at all. But not selling out early is quite different from selling nothing: Rosa may have sold half her goods. D names exactly that overreach. The correct answer is therefore D.
short_explanation: Staying late only shows Rosa did not sell out early; it is an overreach to conclude she sold nothing at all.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
The Kurrajong Gazette claims that the new footbridge over Sandy Creek has reduced car traffic on Miller Road.

Which one of the following pieces of evidence, if true, would best support the newspaper's claim?
option_a: Council traffic counters recorded thirty per cent fewer cars on Miller Road in the three months after the footbridge opened than in the three months before.
option_b: A shop owner on Miller Road says the street feels quieter than it used to.
option_c: The footbridge cost less to build than the council expected.
option_d: The number of people walking across the footbridge grows every week.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: Measured counts comparing traffic before and after the bridge opened give direct, objective evidence that car traffic on Miller Road has fallen.","B":"One person's impression that the street \"feels quieter\" is a weak anecdote, not a measurement of traffic.","C":"The cost of building the bridge says nothing about traffic on Miller Road.","D":"More people walking across the bridge does not show that fewer cars are using Miller Road - the walkers might never have driven."}
worked_solution: The claim is about car traffic on Miller Road going down, so the best evidence measures exactly that. Option A does: official counters, a clear before-and-after comparison, and a large measured drop. B is a single impression, C is about money, and D measures walkers rather than cars - popularity of the bridge does not prove fewer car trips. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: Official before-and-after traffic counts directly measure the claimed drop in cars; the other options are anecdote or beside the point.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
In Dr Okafor's memory study:

- Every student who slept fewer than eight hours the night before scored below their usual mark on the memory test.
- Ingrid scored above her usual mark.
- Callum slept fewer than eight hours.

Based on the above information, which one of the following must be true?
option_a: Callum scored above his usual mark on the memory test.
option_b: Every student who slept eight hours or more scored above their usual mark.
option_c: Ingrid did not sleep fewer than eight hours, and Callum scored below his usual mark.
option_d: Ingrid slept fewer than eight hours the night before.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"This must be false: Callum slept fewer than eight hours, so the first statement means he scored below his usual mark.","B":"The rule only tells us about students who slept fewer than eight hours; it promises nothing about those who slept more.","C":"Correct: If Ingrid had slept fewer than eight hours she would have scored below her usual mark, but she scored above it, so she did not; and Callum, who did sleep fewer than eight hours, must have scored below his.","D":"This must be false: sleeping fewer than eight hours would force a below-usual score, but Ingrid scored above her usual mark."}
worked_solution: Apply the rule both ways. Callum slept fewer than eight hours, so the rule directly forces his score below his usual mark. Ingrid scored above her usual mark, which the rule makes impossible for anyone who slept fewer than eight hours, so she must have slept at least eight. Option B reverses the rule, which only speaks about short sleepers, and A and D contradict the facts. The correct answer is therefore C.
short_explanation: The rule forces Callum below his usual mark, and Ingrid's above-usual score proves she did not sleep fewer than eight hours.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Every Friday this term, the school canteen has sold out of vegetable rolls by lunchtime. Every Friday is also the day the canteen opens half an hour earlier than usual. Ruby concludes that opening earlier is what causes the vegetable rolls to sell out.

Which one of the following, if true, would most weaken Ruby's conclusion?
option_a: On Fridays the canteen also runs a special promotion offering the vegetable rolls at half price.
option_b: The canteen manager says she enjoys opening the canteen early on Fridays.
option_c: Vegetable rolls are the most expensive item the canteen sells.
option_d: Students say the vegetable rolls taste better than the meat pies.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: This gives a different cause present only on Fridays - the half-price promotion - that better explains the sell-out, so opening early may just be a coincidence.","B":"The manager's feelings about opening early do not affect whether early opening causes the sell-out.","C":"Price relative to other items does not explain why rolls sell out specifically on Fridays.","D":"Taste might explain general popularity but not the Friday-only pattern, and it does not compete with the early-opening claim."}
worked_solution: Ruby sees that early opening and selling out happen together on Fridays and assumes one causes the other. To weaken this, we need another factor that is also unique to Fridays and better explains the sell-out. The half-price promotion in option A does exactly that, showing the link to early opening may be a coincidence. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: A rival Friday-only cause (the half-price promotion) undercuts the claim that early opening causes the sell-out.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
At Minerva Public School, students who play a musical instrument tend to get higher marks in maths than students who do not.

**Eli:** "This shows that learning an instrument makes you better at maths. The school should give every student music lessons to raise maths marks."

**Sana:** "Not so fast. It could be that families who can afford instrument lessons also pay for extra maths tutoring. The instrument might not be the reason at all."

Whose reasoning is correct?
option_d: Neither Eli nor Sana
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Eli treats a correlation as proof of cause and ignores other explanations, so his reasoning is flawed.","B":"Correct: Sana correctly points out a possible common cause (family wealth funding both lessons and tutoring), showing Eli has not established that the instrument causes the higher marks.","C":"Eli's reasoning is not sound, so 'both' cannot be correct.","D":"Sana's point is logically sound, so 'neither' is wrong."}
worked_solution: Eli sees that instrument-playing and high maths marks occur together and concludes the instrument causes the marks, which is a cause-versus-correlation error. Sana identifies a plausible confounding factor - wealthier families paying for both instruments and maths tutoring - which shows the instrument may not be the cause. Sana's reasoning is sound and Eli's is not. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: Sana spots a common cause behind both facts; Eli mistakes correlation for causation.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Four friends - Mele, Jae, Otis and Freya - each chose one different sport for the carnival: sprint, relay, high jump or discus.

- Jae chose relay.
- Mele did not choose a running event (sprint or relay).
- Otis chose sprint.
- Freya did not choose discus.

Based on the above information, which one of the following must be true?
option_d: Jae chose high jump.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: Otis took sprint and Jae took relay, so Mele and Freya share high jump and discus; Freya did not take discus, so Freya took high jump and Mele took discus.","B":"Jae chose relay, so Otis - who chose sprint - cannot also have relay; this is false.","C":"Otis chose sprint, so Freya could not; Freya took high jump instead.","D":"Jae chose relay, not high jump, so this is false."}
worked_solution: Jae took relay and Otis took sprint, so the two running events are used up. Mele avoids running events, which is consistent, and Mele and Freya must share high jump and discus. Freya did not choose discus, so Freya took high jump and Mele took discus. The unique assignment is Otis sprint, Jae relay, Freya high jump, Mele discus. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: With sprint and relay taken, Mele and Freya split high jump and discus; Freya avoids discus, so Mele took discus.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Five houses in a row are painted five different colours: red, blue, green, white and yellow.

- The green house is somewhere to the left of the white house.
- The blue house is exactly in the middle (third from either end).
- The red house is at one of the two ends.
- The yellow house is immediately to the right of the green house.

Based on the above information, which one of the following must be true?
option_a: The white house is second from the left.
option_b: The white house is at an end.
option_c: The red house is to the left of the blue house.
option_d: The yellow house is not at either end.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"In the only valid arrangement white is in position 4, not position 2, so this is false.","B":"White ends up in position 4, which is not an end, so this is false.","C":"Red is forced to position 5, to the right of blue at position 3, so red is not to the left of blue - this is false.","D":"Correct: green-yellow must be adjacent and cannot sit in positions 4-5 (white would have nowhere to go), so they take positions 1-2, putting yellow in position 2, never at an end."}
worked_solution: Blue is fixed at position 3. Green-yellow must be adjacent with green left of yellow, so the pair is (1,2) or (4,5). If they took (4,5) then white, which must be right of green, would have no free position, so green-yellow take (1,2). Red must then be at position 5 (position 1 is green), leaving white at position 4. The unique order is green, yellow, blue, white, red, so yellow sits at position 2 - not an end. The correct answer is therefore D.
short_explanation: The only order is green, yellow, blue, white, red, so yellow is at position 2, never an end.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:

worked_solution: Bilal plays neither draughts nor backgammon, so Bilal must play chess. Since each game is played by only one person, no one else can play chess, so Viktor cannot play chess. Ananya and Viktor then split draughts and backgammon, both orders being possible, so only option A is ruled out. The correct answer is therefore A.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Six lockers in a row are numbered 1 to 6 from left to right. Four of them belong to Eli, Sana, Ruby and Otis; the other two are empty.

- Eli's locker number is smaller than Sana's.
- Ruby's locker is number 4.
- Otis has locker number 1.
- The two empty lockers are next to each other.

Which one of the following is not possible?
option_a: Eli has locker 2 and Sana has locker 3.
option_c: Eli has locker 3 and Sana has locker 5.
option_d: The empty lockers are 2 and 3.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Possible: Otis=1, Eli=2, Sana=3, Ruby=4 leaves 5 and 6 empty and adjacent.","B":"Possible: this matches option A's arrangement, so empties at 5 and 6 can happen.","C":"Correct: Eli=3 and Sana=5 leaves lockers 2 and 6 empty, but 2 and 6 are not next to each other, breaking the adjacency rule - so this is impossible.","D":"Possible: empties at 2 and 3 put Eli and Sana in 5 and 6 with Eli=5, Sana=6, satisfying Eli smaller than Sana."}
worked_solution: Otis is fixed at 1 and Ruby at 4, so Eli and Sana occupy two of {2,3,5,6}, and the two empty lockers must be adjacent. In option C, Eli=3 and Sana=5 leaves lockers 2 and 6 empty, but 2 and 6 are not next to each other, breaking the adjacency rule. Every other option leaves a valid adjacent empty pair. The correct answer is therefore C.
short_explanation: Eli=3, Sana=5 leaves empties at 2 and 6, which are not adjacent, violating the rule.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
At the sports carnival, the rule for the relay team is:

**If a student runs in the relay, then that student has attended every training session.**

Freya did not attend every training session.

Based on the above information, which one of the following must be true?
option_b: Freya did not run in the relay.
option_c: Every student who attended all sessions ran in the relay.
option_d: Some students who ran in the relay missed a session.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"This contradicts the rule: a relay runner must have attended all sessions, and Freya did not.","B":"Correct: by the contrapositive, if a student did not attend every session, that student did not run in the relay; Freya missed a session, so she did not run in the relay.","C":"The rule says relay runners attended all sessions, not that all attenders ran - this reverses the rule.","D":"The rule guarantees every relay runner attended all sessions, so no relay runner missed one."}
worked_solution: The rule states: relay runner implies attended every session. The contrapositive - which must also be true - is: did not attend every session implies did not run in the relay. Freya did not attend every session, so she did not run in the relay. The correct answer is therefore B.
short_explanation: By contrapositive, missing a session means Freya could not have run in the relay.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
Mr Whitlam tells his class:

**If you finish the extension worksheet, then you may use the computers at lunch.**

At lunchtime, Bilal is using the computers.

Bilal concludes: "Because I am using the computers, I must have finished the extension worksheet."

Which one of the following sentences best shows the mistake Bilal has made?
option_a: Just because finishing the worksheet allows computer use, it does not mean that using the computers proves the worksheet was finished.
option_b: Just because Bilal is using the computers, it does not mean that the computers are working.
option_c: Just because the worksheet is extension work, it does not mean that it is difficult.
option_d: Just because Mr Whitlam made a rule, it does not mean that everyone follows it.
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: the rule only guarantees the worksheet leads to computer use, not the reverse; students might be allowed on the computers for other reasons, so using them does not prove Bilal finished the worksheet - this is the converse error.","B":"Whether the computers work is irrelevant to the logical flaw in Bilal's reasoning.","C":"The difficulty of the worksheet has nothing to do with the reversed conditional.","D":"Whether people obey the rule is not the point; Bilal's error is reversing the if-then direction."}
worked_solution: The rule says finishing the worksheet is enough to allow computer use, but it does not say it is the only way to earn computer use. Bilal reverses the rule, assuming computer use proves the worksheet was done - a converse error. Option A names exactly this flaw. The correct answer is therefore A.
short_explanation: Bilal reverses the rule; computer use does not prove the worksheet was finished (converse error).

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text:
In a class, every student is in at least one of two clubs: the chess club or the art club.

- 18 students are in the chess club.
- 15 students are in the art club.

question_text: I have a three-digit code for my locker. I remember these clues: the first digit is double the third digit; the second digit is 3; and all three digits add up to 12. What is my locker code?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: let the third digit be d, so the first is 2d and the second is 3; then 2d + 3 + d = 12 gives d = 3 and first = 6, so the code is 633.","B":"632 has digits summing to 11, not 12.","C":"336 puts the doubled digit last, reversing the first-digit clue.","D":"663 makes the second digit 6 instead of 3."}
worked_solution: Let the third digit be d. The first digit is 2d and the second digit is 3. The digits add to 12, so 2d + 3 + d = 12, giving 3d = 9 and d = 3. Then the first digit is 6. The code is 6, 3, 3. The correct answer is therefore A.

subject: Thinking Skills
question_text: At Zainab's market stall, prices are written in a secret code where each letter always stands for the same digit. She has written FIG as 4 2 7 and FUN as 4 9 5. Using the same code, what number does the word GUN stand for?
option_explanations_json: {"A":"Correct: from FIG, G = 7; from FUN, U = 9 and N = 5; so GUN = 7, 9, 5 = 795.","B":"725 keeps G = 7 but wrongly makes U = 2 from the word FIG.","C":"975 reverses the digits, putting N before G.","D":"792 swaps N for the digit 2 taken from the wrong word."}
worked_solution: Match letters to digits from the two coded words. FIG = 4 2 7 means F = 4, I = 2, G = 7. FUN = 4 9 5 means F = 4, U = 9, N = 5. So GUN = G, U, N = 7, 9, 5 = 795. The correct answer is therefore A.

After a wild night of storms, the little beach town of Cabarita Point wakes to a shoreline littered with strange objects. You are the first person on the sand, and something half-buried near the rocks catches your eye.

- show how your discovery changes your day, or your whole town
- use the sights, sounds and smells of the beach after the storm

Every school holidays, you stay with your grandpa on a small island in the Hawkesbury River, where the only way back to the mainland is the evening ferry. This visit, Grandpa keeps saying something you do not understand - until the very last day.

- build events across the visit that slowly reveal his meaning
- make the final moment feel earned, not sudden

subject: Writing
question_text:
**First Light on Mars Station Mirambeena**

The year is 2091. Your family has just moved from Adelaide to Mars Station Mirambeena, Australia's first settlement on another planet, and today was your first full day there.

Write a diary entry that begins with this exact sentence: *Dear Diary, this morning I watched Earth rise over the crater rim, and it was smaller than my thumbnail.*

- describe one everyday activity, like school or dinner, that works very differently on Mars
- share what you miss about Earth and what amazes you here
- end with a hope or a worry about tomorrow

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Save the Saltwater Pool**

Barragoola Shire Council says the ninety-year-old ocean pool at Pelican Point costs too much to repair and should be filled in and replaced with a car park. Locals are furious: the pool is where generations learned to swim, where the dawn swimmers meet, and where the nippers train. A petition is growing, and the council will vote next month.

Write a persuasive piece for the councillors arguing whether the pool should be saved or replaced.

In your writing, you could:
- weigh the repair costs against what the pool gives the community
- include the different people who would be affected by the decision
- open powerfully and end with a clear call to action

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Too Young to Scroll?**

Australians are still arguing about children and social media. Some say accounts for under-13s harm sleep, focus and confidence, and that childhood should happen offline. Others say social media is where modern kids connect, create and learn, and that banning it just pushes it underground. A national kids' news website has invited Year 6 students to have their say.

Write a persuasive piece for the website arguing whether children under 13 should have their own social media accounts.

In your writing, you could:
- take a clear stand and support it with evidence from kids' real lives
- respect and rebut the strongest argument on the other side
- use persuasive devices that work on readers your own age

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Master of One, or Jack of All?**

Some people spend years mastering a single pursuit - one sport, one instrument, one obsession - while others happily sample everything from chess to surf lifesaving without settling. Coaches, teachers and parents disagree about which path serves a young person better.

Write a discursive piece exploring whether it is better to specialise in one pursuit or to try many. You do not have to pick a winner, but you must weigh the perspectives thoughtfully.

In your writing, you could:
- explore at least two different perspectives, each with convincing examples
- use a considered personal voice that wonders as well as asserts
- reach a thoughtful conclusion, even if it remains balanced

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Behind the Glass**

A new wildlife park has opened an hour from your town, and it has restarted an old argument. Supporters say modern zoos fund conservation, breed endangered species and turn visitors into lifelong animal advocates. Critics say no enclosure, however clever, is a life in the wild - and that watching captive animals teaches the wrong lesson entirely.

Write a discursive piece exploring the question: do animals belong in zoos?

In your writing, you could:
- present the strongest versions of at least two viewpoints
- include examples of particular animals, zoos or moments that shaped your thinking
- end with a reflection that acknowledges what remains unresolved

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Vote One: You**

Elections for school captain at Girraween Heights Public School are next week. Every candidate gets exactly two minutes at Friday's assembly in front of the whole school - students from Kindergarten to Year 6, the teachers, and the principal, Mr Salib. You have decided to run.

Write the speech you would deliver at assembly.

In your writing, you could:
- open with a hook that makes six hundred people look up
- make promises that are specific and actually achievable
- use spoken-word devices such as direct address, repetition and a memorable closing line

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Dear Ranger Okafor**

Wattlebrook Wildlife Rescue is choosing five Year 6 students for its new Junior Ranger program these summer holidays. Junior Rangers will help feed orphaned wombat joeys, monitor the flying-fox colony and teach visitors about native animals. To apply, students must write a letter to the head ranger, Amara Okafor, explaining why they should be chosen.

Write your application letter to Ranger Okafor.

In your writing, you could:
- follow the conventions of a formal letter, from greeting to sign-off
- give evidence of your qualities, skills and any experience with animals or helping others
- show what you hope to learn and what you would give back to the program

subject: Writing
question_text:
**From Scraps to Soil**

Your school has just launched a composting program: every class has a green scraps bucket, and behind the canteen sit three new worm farms and a compost bay. The principal wants an article on the school website so that every family understands the program - and so more of them start composting at home.

Write an informative article explaining the composting program: how it works, why it matters, and how students and families can take part.

In your writing, you could:
- organise your information under clear headings or in logical sections
- explain the science simply and accurately, defining any technical words
- keep to facts and explanations rather than opinions and persuasion

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Emu Causes Chaos on Main Street**

Yesterday morning, an emu named Bruce escaped from a hobby farm on the edge of Tallawarra and spent two hours strolling down the main street - inspecting the bakery window, halting the school bus, and posing for tourists' photos before being gently herded home by the vet and two volunteers.

Write a newspaper report about the incident for the *Tallawarra Times*.

In your writing, you could:
- explain what happened
- describe the impact
- include comments from different people

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Mystery Knitter Strikes Again**

Residents of the seaside town of Kurraburra woke on Sunday to find every statue, bench and bike rack in Memorial Park wrapped in brightly coloured knitting - tiny beanies on the war memorial soldiers, a striped scarf on the anchor, even booties on the flagpole. Nobody saw a thing. It is the third time this year, and the council is divided over whether to investigate or to say thank you.

Write a newspaper report about the mystery for the *Kurraburra Courier*.

In your writing, you could:
- explain what happened
- describe the impact
- include comments from different people

subject: Writing
question_text:
**Surviving Your First School Camp**

Next term, the Year 5 students of your school head off on their very first three-day camp: cabins, bushwalks, a giant swing, and seventy-two hours away from home. The teachers have asked Year 6 - the camp veterans - to prepare an advice sheet to hand out at the information night.

Write an advice sheet for Year 5 students preparing for their first school camp.

In your writing, you could:
- organise your advice under clear, helpful headings
- give practical dos and don'ts, with a reason for each
- keep the tone friendly and reassuring, especially about homesickness

subject: Writing
question_text:
**The Note in the Library Book**

Tucked inside a second-hand atlas donated to your school library, you find a folded, yellowing note in careful handwriting:

*"To whoever finds this - I hid it where the river bends twice and the ghost gums lean together. It is not treasure exactly, but it mattered to me more than anything. Please look after it, whatever you decide. - E.W., January 1974."*

Decide what this note means, and respond to it in whatever written form fits your idea best - a story, a diary entry, a letter back through time, a newspaper report, or another form you can justify. Name your chosen form at the top of your response.

In your writing, you could:
- use the details of the note: the hiding place, the date, the mysterious 'it', and E.W. themselves
- choose a form that suits your idea, and follow that form's conventions faithfully
- reveal (or deliberately withhold) what was hidden, and why it mattered
